title: practical english usage - Part 307
content:
- What time did I come to see you in the office yesterday?

was or will be at the time of the movement. Compare: We   can use come for a movement to a place where the speaker or hearer already 2 speaker's/hearer's           past    or  future     position In 1577, he went to study in Rome.

I want to go and live in Greece. Let's go and see Daniel and Chloe.

We use go for movements to other places.

(on the phone): Can I come and see you?

When did you come to live here?

‘ Maria, would you come here, please? ‘ I'm coming.' (     .. . I'm going.) We   use come for movements to the place where the speaker or hearer is.

1 speaker's/hearer's           position 424 c ome     and   go  ______        ______ I must buy some new clothes; I haven't got anything to wear.

or an article / a piece of clothing.

Clothes has no singular; instead of a clothe, we  say something to wear Clothes (pronounced /kləuðz/)   are things you wear: skirt, trousers, etc.

Could you pass me a cloth? I’ ve spilt some milk on the floor.

A cloth is a piece of material used for cleaning, covering things, etc.

His suits were made of the most expensive cloth.

more common to say material or fabric.) making clothes, curtains, soft furnishings and so on. (In informal English, it is Cloth (pronounced /kloθ/) is material made from wool, cotton, etc, used for 423 cloth      and   clothes letters, bank accounts, meetings, etc.

We   close roads, railways, etc (channels of communication). And we  close (= end) Shut your mouth! (a rude way of saying 'Be quiet!’ ) Close your mouth,  please, (dentist to patient) Compare: As we watched, he closed his eyes for the last time.

more common in a formal style.

We   prefer close for slow movements (like flowers closing at night), and close is 3 cases    where     close      is  preferred Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  425  comic and comical For the grammatical meaning of complement, see Language Terminology.

pronounced /'kɔmplɪment/.

Both nouns are pronounced /'kɔmplimɔnt/. Both verbs are I must compliment you on your exam results.

My new hairstyle is getting a lot of compliments.

a similar meaning.

A  compliment is an expression of praise or admiration. The  verb compliment has They have very different personalities, but they complement each other.

Dinner at Antonio's - the perfect complement to your evening at the opera.

The  verb complement has a similar meaning.

A  complement adds to the quality of something, or goes well with it.

verb   )                 ____________ 426 complement             and   compliment             (noun    and a comical expression Comical is a rather old-fashioned word meaning ‘ funny’ .

comic verse comic opera Shakespeare's comic technique Comic is the normal adjective for artistic comedy.

(     Originally I came from Hungary...) Originally I come from Hungary, but I've lived here for twenty years.

She comes from Scotland, but her mother's Welsh.

Come from is used (in the present) to say where people’ s homes are or were.

Go straight on till you come to a crossroads.

Come to can mean arrive at.

5 come      to;  come      from He waited till four o'clock, but she didn't come.

happens in stories.

come for movements to the place where he/she is (or was or will be). This often hearer), that person can become the centre of our attention. In that case, we  use Sometimes when we  are talking about somebody else (not the speaker or 4 somebody        else's    position We're going to a concert tonight. Would you like to come with us?

hearer’ s, even if go is used for the movement itself.

Come (with) can be used to talk about joining a movement of the speaker’ s/ 3 joining     a  movement vocabulary •  429  control expressions like passport/customs control.

However, the noun control is used with the meaning of ‘ inspection point’  in not (     ... to control the steering) I took the car to the garage and asked them to have a look at the steering.
